Author: Mike Fulk
Title: The AI Hype Machine
Text:
The AI Hype Machine
Coding Chat Bots Edition
BLUF: I won’t be using them anytime soon for any real work. The very limited set of things they seem to help with are a small fraction of professional software development.
Week 2 of unemployment starts today! What have I been working on besides the YouTube channel for the podcast you ask? I signed up a few weeks back for a couple of AI tester/question builder positions. Funny enough, I was recruited on LinkedIn and then with some searching I found another that was similar.
Here is how it worked at a high level. Both of these organizations were looking for people to do manual work around training GenAI tools. One was asking for novel questions that were not to be found online anywhere, related to specific topics of interest. They wanted difficult to solve, unique questions that would not have shown up in existing training data.
Both also wanted help with testing these novel questions, putting them into an LLM chat bot and reviewing the output. Since my area of expertise is in all things computer related (programming, data engineering, data analysis, scaling, etc.) I worked on these kinds of technical questions to get some peek into the GenAI training world. The testing tasks paid $15–35 per question and the generation $35–50. Not an unlimited pool here so this isn’t something to get rich from.
Now to the punch line. Making the questions wasn’t worth my time or really interesting to me, I quickly discovered. But trying out the testing of these questions with a chat bot did get my attention. Why? Well the way it worked for me at least was as follows.
- Get a question about writing some code to solve a problem or editing some code that doesn’t work properly.
- Get the reply from the LLM and review it.
- Take the chat bot code and try to run it in a dev environment.
- Evaluate where the mistakes are and how they’d be fixed.
- Evaluate what dependencies the chat bot forgot to bring up.
This was interesting for a couple of reasons.
I got to see languages I don’t usually code in and set up environments quickly to use them
- Rust I had never even read or looked at before but it seems fun. I put it on my to do list to get some simple rust practice and knowledge.
- Go also. I need to get some simple Go practice, even though I had done enough in the past to be able to read it, I hadn’t thought in that language before.
- My XCode skills had rusted considerably. I put this on my list also!
Ok, besides just getting a lot of fun practice reading languages besides Python and Java, what else did I learn?
- Of the 30ish coding problems I ran, NONE OF THEM WORKED without edits.
- There were ALWAYS ERRORS even in seemingly syntactically correct code that was well organized and named.
- Logic mistakes were not easily corrected by the chat bot but sometimes identified.
- Simple input prompts with minimal requirements could get a workable set of template code.
- A template would still need to be reviewed to be understood by a programmer that wanted to use it.
- Working with template code MAY BE SOMEWHAT USEFUL (e.g., if you’re lazy and/or a bad typist) but there are better ways to build templates if you really plan to use the code later.
- A complex input prompt with many requirements leads to the the chat bot telling you to kick rocks basically, as it only provides the template boiler plate code anyway (with an explanation about lowering your expectations in some cases).
- The chat bot will tell you in words how to test your code better than it will write a test case (again these always have errors).
- They seem to do best with what look like homework cases from a University course that have likely been seen before in training data.
- They struggle to work on meaningful or new applications that will serve a customer (e.g., secure, privacy, hardened to exceptions and faults).
That’s it! I’m going to put this at the top also. My bottom line is I’ll take your money but I don’t find a lot of utility in using these coding chat bots other than exposure to new languages I hadn’t used before. Maybe someone could make a learn Rust course with an LLM and I’d run through it, where I debug the chatbots mistakes as I learn the language?
Nah!!!
